findings.description: Stored XSS: event['display_name'] (and the action/created_at cells) are interpolated into HTML table rows via f-string with no HTML escaping (CWE-79, OWASP A05:2025); an attacker-controlled username like <script>...</script> persisted in the DB executes in the admin's browser when the activity report is viewed, yielding admin-session theft.

The caller-supplied 'title' is injected into PAGE_TEMPLATE via .format() with no escaping; if any caller derives title from user/request input this is a second stored/reflected XSS sink (CWE-79), and the template ships no Content-Security-Policy or output encoding to mitigate it.

save_report passes 'out_path' directly to open(out_path,'w') with no path normalization or base-directory confinement; if out_path is influenced by request input this is path traversal / arbitrary file write or overwrite (CWE-22/CWE-73, A01:2025), potentially writing attacker-controlled HTML to a web-served path.

fetch_events + render_activity_report load every event in the date range into memory and concatenate all rows into one HTML string with no LIMIT/pagination; a wide date range over a large events table is a resource-exhaustion/DoS vector (CWE-400, A06 missing rate-limiting) — algorithmic DoS treated as security per scope.

db_path flows unvalidated into sqlite3.connect(); if ever caller/request-influenced, an attacker-controlled path (incl. SQLite URI filenames) could read or create arbitrary database files (A01) — low risk since db_path is typically a config constant.

logger.info writes the attacker-influenceable 'title' into the log unsanitized; embedded CR/LF could forge or split log lines (log injection, CWE-117). No secrets/PII are logged, so impact is limited.

save_report does no exception handling; an OSError/IOError propagates raw to the caller and, with DEBUG enabled in a web context, can leak a stack trace / internal path to users (CWE-209, A10:2025 Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions).

Non-security correctness bug (deferred to logic critic): docstring claims '[start, end] both boundary days included' but the query uses created_at < ? making 'end' exclusive, so events on the final day are silently dropped.
